clean_text: They ask me Alhamdulillah. Continuing in the chapter of patience, patience as we are going to learn and as we have learned somewhat in this chapter is far more and far more broad. It stretches far beyond merely patience in one thing as opposed to another or patience you know a lot of times people will gauge patience as only being patients with regards to one or two things right so if somebody is very slow to anger for instance or if someone is very quick to anger this is a part of patience. This is not all of patience. Patience is far more broad than that. Kind of character. Often times we kind of reduce good character as just smiling. That's not good character per se. That's a a very small part of a much bigger thing that is good character. Good good character sometimes it is part of good character not to smile. It can be right. Good character is that a person gets angry for the sake of Allah. loves for the sake of Allah. gives for the sake of Allah, withholds for the sake of Allah, is truthful, is courageous, right? Is generous. This is all part of character. reducing these huge broad terms to very small acts or individual isolated things of that makes it very superficial kind of like worship which we've discussed before say what is worship say well worship is you know making salah and fasting in Ramadan and reading the Quran and you know that's pretty much it. That is an incredibly simplistic view of what worship is, right? Those are just instances of worship. As a matter of fact, the majority of a of a man or woman's worship will not be in those three things. Worship is anything that pleases Allah subhanahu wa'ta'ala. Anything that pleases Allah. Telling the truth is worship. an individual even a person's sleep, even a person's diet can be worship. And so as we'll learn, patience is far more broad than one or two isolated acts. patience is a very broad term that has many aspects to it and for this reason he famously was quoted as saying that patience is to a man's religion as one's head is to his body. Right? Patience is to a man's. Religion as one's head is to his body. Without a person's head, they have no life even if the rest of the body is there. Likewise is the issue of patience. We stopped at the 155th verse of Bakari. When he begins a chapter on a particular topic, he will usually begin with a verse of the Quran or verses of the Quran. This will act as a prelude and the basis upon which all the following narrations will orbit and will circle. And so, , the choices that he makes for those verses, the select verses that he opens a chapter with, that he begins a chapter with, they are extremely precise. Each one of the verses that he chooses, he has chosen it for its unique contribution to the general meaning of the chapter. Yesterday we had kind of an introduction into the verse number 155. The verse has a lot of meanings which is why today we will speak about one of those meanings and tomorrow complete those meanings inshallah and that is the statement of Allah which translates to and indeed verily we are going to test you test you all with a portion of fear and hunger and a lack of wealth and bodies and lives. lives and fruits and glad tidings for the patient. Starting with this statement of Allah, we see this setup throughout the Quran, the wow, the lamb followed by the what is referred to as which is the response of the verily and indeed and so forth. What does this mean? Whenever we see this lamb this wow after it is a lamb this is called this means that Allah is swearing upon something. It's as if Allah is saying, "I swear by your Lord that verily indeed we are going to test you all." Now, if we understand just how concrete this statement of Allah is, that there's no escape. It is going to happen. If a person is a believer in any way, even if their belief is very very weak, they must get a portion of testing that comes with that. The prophet sallall.aii wasallam mentions that Wasam. He said sallallah.aii wasallam that the most tribulated, the most tried, the most tested men from amongst mankind are the prophets, followed by those who are most similar to the prophets, followed by those most similar to them. Each person will be tested based on the strength of his eman. If his eman is very strong, he will have an additional portion of tribulation. And if his eman is lesser than that, then his test will be proportionate to his patience. We ask Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala and Muhammad. Alhamdulillah.

summary: This lecture discusses the chapter on patience from an Islamic perspective, emphasizing that patience is a broad concept far beyond simple tolerance or anger management. The speaker explains that patience, like good character and worship, encompasses many aspects of life and behavior - being truthful, generous, courageous, and acting for the sake of Allah. The lecture then transitions to an explanation of Quran 2:155, which discusses how Allah tests believers with fear, hunger, loss of wealth, and lives, emphasizing that testing is a certainty for all believers proportional to the strength of their faith. The speaker notes that the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) taught that prophets face the greatest trials, followed by those most similar to them.
suggested_title: Understanding Patience and Divine Testing: Commentary on Quran 2:155
catchy_title: Is Patience Just About Anger? Think Again
